script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Wednesday — here is your QQQM recap. The QQQ portfolio closed one point two three percent down on the session. It was a split tape, with semiconductor names leading in opposite directions and geopolitical pressure weighing on the broader index.

Apple and Broadcom just locked in the largest chip manufacturing deal in United States history. Thirty billion dollars. A multi-year agreement running through twenty thirty-one, covering production of more than fifteen billion custom chips on American soil. That number alone reframes the domestic semiconductor conversation entirely.

The broad market sold off Wednesday as oil prices spiked on renewed tension between the United States and Iran. President Trump signaled the ceasefire may be over, warning conditions would deteriorate further if Iran continued attacking ships. The Nasdaq outperformed on the back of strength in artificial intelligence-related names, even as the Dow logged its worst session in nearly a month. No major economic data prints were released Wednesday, leaving geopolitics as the dominant macro driver.

The headline of Wednesday's session belongs to Apple and Broadcom. Apple confirmed a multi-year partnership with Broadcom valued at more than thirty billion dollars, covering custom silicon components and wireless technologies through twenty thirty-one. The deal includes production of over fifteen billion chips, with Broadcom committing to a one point five billion dollar expansion of its Fort Collins, Colorado facility. This is the largest domestic chip manufacturing agreement ever announced by an American technology company, and it lands at a moment when supply chain sovereignty is a boardroom and policy priority. For Broadcom, the market responded immediately — shares jumped nearly four percent on the session. For Apple, the read is more nuanced. The stock closed essentially flat, reflecting that the market views this as strategic infrastructure spending rather than a near-term earnings catalyst. What it does signal is Apple's deepening commitment to vertical integration in silicon, a theme that has underpinned the stock's premium valuation for several years. Analysts covering both names will be watching for any margin commentary tied to the deal structure in the next earnings cycle.

Turning to Nvidia, the portfolio's heaviest holding at seven point six percent. Nvidia closed at two hundred four dollars and twelve cents, up four point three eight percent on the session. That is the single largest positive contributor to portfolio performance Wednesday. The gain came as artificial intelligence names broadly outperformed, with the Nasdaq finding support even as the Dow fell. Jim Cramer flagged a separate risk Wednesday — the wave of new stock offerings and debt issuance hitting the market — though his commentary was not Nvidia-specific. The SK Hynix Nasdaq listing, expected July tenth, is worth monitoring for Nvidia holders given the deep integration of SK Hynix's high bandwidth memory inside Nvidia's AI accelerators. Watch for any pricing or demand signals around that debut.

Apple closed at three hundred thirteen dollars and thirty-nine cents, up zero point two three percent on the session. The Broadcom deal, detailed above, was the defining Apple story Wednesday. The market's muted reaction to the stock itself suggests investors are treating this as a long-cycle capital commitment rather than an immediate revenue event. The next major catalyst for Apple remains its product cycle and any forward guidance on artificial intelligence feature monetization.

Alphabet closed at three hundred sixty-one dollars and ninety-two cents, down one point two four percent on the session. A notable development: reports indicate Warren Buffett's successor at Berkshire Hathaway, Greg Abel, has deployed more than twenty billion dollars of Berkshire's cash into Alphabet — described as one of the boldest bets of the post-Buffett era. Despite that institutional vote of confidence, shares finished lower Wednesday, caught in the broad market selloff.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred forty-eight dollars and eighty cents, down three point six five percent on the session. No company-specific news drove the move — this appears to be sector rotation pressure and risk-off sentiment tied to geopolitical uncertainty. Micron holders will want to watch the SK Hynix listing Thursday for any read-through on high bandwidth memory demand.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-three dollars and thirty-four cents, down zero point eight eight percent. The decline was modest relative to some peers and reflects the broader market tone rather than any company-specific catalyst. No material news crossed Wednesday.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-three dollars and sixty-two cents, down zero point two two percent — essentially flat on the session. Consumer discretionary names were mixed Wednesday, and Amazon's modest decline reflects no identifiable company-specific pressure. Cloud and advertising trends remain the primary watch items heading into next earnings.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred seventeen dollars and forty cents, down six point two eight percent — one of the sharpest declines in the primary holdings Wednesday. No single headline drove the move, but the selloff mirrors pressure across the semiconductor space outside of artificial intelligence flagship names. Advanced Micro Devices has been a volatile position, and Wednesday's session reinforced that pattern.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and six cents, down six point one two percent. The Iran-driven oil spike may have added modest headwind to electric vehicle sentiment, though the primary driver appears to be broader risk-off selling. Tesla remains one of the more geopolitically sensitive names in the portfolio given its energy and international exposure.

Intel closed at one hundred ten dollars and twenty-four cents, down nine point seven nine percent Wednesday — the steepest single-session decline among primary holdings. That is a significant move, and it drags on portfolio performance given Intel's two point nine percent weight. No specific earnings catalyst was flagged, but Intel has been under sustained competitive pressure in both data center and consumer processor markets. This is a name to watch closely for any analyst commentary or guidance revision in the days ahead.

Turning briefly to a secondary holding with direct news relevance — Broadcom closed at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and sixty-nine cents, up three point nine six percent. As discussed, the Apple chip deal was the catalyst. Broadcom's gain Wednesday partially offsets weakness elsewhere in the semiconductor space and represents one of the cleaner fundamental moves of the session.

Three items to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, the SK Hynix Nasdaq listing is expected Thursday, July tenth. As the dominant supplier of high bandwidth memory for artificial intelligence accelerators, any pricing or trading dynamics around that debut carry read-through implications for Nvidia and Micron holders. Second, watch for any follow-on analyst commentary on the Apple-Broadcom thirty billion dollar chip deal — specifically whether margin guidance for either company is revised. Third, monitor crude oil prices and any further developments in the United States-Iran situation. Wednesday's oil spike was the primary macro driver of the selloff, and further escalation would likely pressure the growth and consumer discretionary names in this portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the SK Hynix debut Thursday — how that stock trades out of the gate may set the tone for the entire artificial intelligence supply chain into the end of the week.
